concerns.text: The plan proposes using '200 PCA components' derived from a 200x200 connectivity matrix as a baseline. This is mathematically tautological: PCA on a 200x200 matrix yields at most 199 non-zero components (rank deficiency). Using these components as a 'baseline' to compare against entropy features (which are also 200 dimensions) creates a circular comparison where the baseline is a linear transformation of the same raw time-series data used to derive entropy, failing to test if entropy adds *non-linear* information beyond linear correlation.

The plan calculates the entropy tolerance parameter `r` as `0.2 * SD` of the *post-scrubbed* time series (FR-010). However, it then standardizes all subjects to a fixed length N=120 (FR-011). If scrubbing removes different numbers of volumes per subject, the SD calculation is based on different effective sample sizes before truncation. More critically, if the 'standardization' involves interpolation to reach N=120, the SD (and thus `r`) becomes a function of the interpolation method, not just the signal complexity, introducing a confound where entropy values are partially determined by the imputation strategy rather than the raw BOLD signal.

The success criterion requires a difference in Pearson correlation (Δr) ≥ 0.05 between Entropy-only and Connectivity-only models. In neuroimaging, linear connectivity (correlation) and Sample Entropy (non-linear complexity) are often highly correlated due to shared variance in signal amplitude and noise. Without a partial correlation analysis or a nested model comparison (e.g., likelihood ratio test) controlling for the linear connectivity features, a simple difference in r may not statistically prove that entropy adds *unique* predictive value, only that it correlates with the outcome.
